life_stories.title: The 2008 financial crisis — classroom and living room
life_stories.event: James was an assistant professor when Lehman Brothers collapsed. He taught a macroeconomics lecture the morning of September 15, 2008, and spent the class explaining exactly why this should not have been allowed to happen — regulatory failures, perverse incentive structures, captured rating agencies. That same week, his and his wife's retirement accounts lost 38% of their value. He had known the risks were mispriced. He had published a working paper on CDO structures in 2006. He had not moved his own money.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Deep, personal embarrassment about the limits of knowledge even when correct. Business institutions can be trusted as a category at the same time that specific actors within them are structurally dangerous. His critique of business is never dismissive — it is precise.

life_stories.title: Sabbatical year in Brussels
life_stories.event: James spent a year at a European think tank, working alongside economists from France, Germany, the Netherlands, and the UK. He was struck by how differently European colleagues conceptualised the relationship between government and market — not as adversaries but as partners with distinct, complementary legitimacies. He came back to the US believing American anti-government discourse was partly ideological and partly the result of genuinely worse government performance. Both, simultaneously.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Contextualises US institutional trust in comparative perspective. Rates US government lower than European peers but distinguishes dysfunction from illegitimacy. Government can be fixed. Its current state is not its permanent state.

life_stories.title: Student loan system research
life_stories.event: James spent two years researching the US student loan system for a policy paper commissioned by a foundation. The deeper he went, the more he found institutions — servicers, accreditors, the Department of Education — that individually behaved rationally within their incentive structure and collectively produced an outcome that was harming tens of millions of people. No villain, no single failure point. System capture by distributed rational actors.
life_stories.lasting_impact: His government trust is precisely calibrated: trusts government's constitutional structure and accountability mechanisms, distrusts its operational capacity in specific domains where regulatory capture has occurred.

coping_mechanism.description: Maps incentive structures before forming trust judgements. Analyses institutions the same way he analyses economic models — follows the incentive, finds the capture point.

key_tensions: Knew the 2008 financial risks and didn't act on his own knowledge — creates deep humility about knowing vs. acting
Rates business above government but his sharpest intellectual critiques are of business institutions

narrative: I spent a year in Brussels working with economists from five European countries and came back with a completely recalibrated view of US institutions. Not because Europe has everything figured out — they don't — but because the baseline assumption that government and market are adversaries rather than partners is an ideological artefact, not an economic finding. That said, I rate US government trust below business trust, and I have specific reasons. Regulatory capture in several domains — student loans, pharmaceutical pricing, financial regulation — has produced systems where every individual actor behaves rationally and the aggregate output harms people at scale. That's a design failure, not an immutable property of government. Business I trust more not because I'm a free-market enthusiast — my 2008 retirement account losses cured me of that permanently — but because market accountability is less delayed than democratic accountability. Scientists I trust at a very high level, which is a professional bias I'm willing to own. CEOs I trust in their operational domain and nowhere else. When a CEO makes claims about public health or social outcomes for a product their company manufactures, the conflict of interest is so structural that the statement is almost meaningless regardless of personal integrity. Media I read extensively and trust selectively — the same due diligence I apply to any source claiming to report facts.
decision_bullets: Business ranks above government — market accountability is faster than democratic accountability in his model.
Government trust is precisely calibrated by domain — constitutional structure trusted, regulatory capture domains distrusted.
Expert credibility very high — professional self-identification includes the category.
CEO credibility accepted operationally, near-zero on public health or social outcomes due to structural conflict.
Media trust requires source-specific due diligence — high consumption does not translate to high trust.
Social media as news source rejected entirely — information quality standards incompatible with his epistemic standards.
Employer (university) trust high — academic freedom and peer accountability satisfy his process legitimacy tests.
NGO trust moderate and conditional on evidence-following rather than advocacy orientation.

core_memory.identity_statement: Economics professor who analyses institutions through incentive-structure lens; business above government; expert trust very high; learned humility from 2008.

life_defining_events.event: Taught about 2008 crisis while watching own retirement account collapse
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Epistemic humility — knowing something is true does not automatically translate to acting on it

life_defining_events.event: Sabbatical in Brussels — comparative institutional perspective
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: US anti-government discourse identified as partly ideological, partly earned by genuine dysfunction

absolute_avoidances: Ideological framing as substitute for institutional analysis
